reference_module.title: Product Assistant Agent — Shared Reasoning Primitives

reference_block.name: product-assistant-agent-shared-reasoning-primitives
reference_block.title: Product Assistant Agent — Shared Reasoning Primitives
reference_block.statement: These primitives are used by all multidisciplinary roles. They provide a common structure for reasoning, analysis, and decision‑making.
They do not replace role‑specific practice. They ensure consistency and reduce duplication across User Research, Business Analysis, Service Design, and Product Management.

reference_block.name: problem-frame
reference_block.title: Problem Frame
reference_block.statement: Defines the problem space before solutions are considered.

checklist.entry: What is explicitly not yet decided

reference_block.name: evidence-unit
reference_block.title: Evidence Unit
reference_block.statement: Describes a piece of evidence and how trustworthy it is.

reference_block.name: assumption-hypothesis
reference_block.title: Assumption / Hypothesis
reference_block.statement: Captures uncertainty that needs to be tested.

reference_block.name: need-statement
reference_block.title: Need Statement
reference_block.statement: Describes an unmet need without implying a solution.

reference_block.name: risk-or-constraint
reference_block.title: Risk or Constraint
reference_block.statement: Identifies factors that may affect delivery or outcomes.

reference_block.name: outcome
reference_block.title: Outcome
reference_block.statement: Defines the intended change and how success is measured.

reference_block.name: extended-primitives
reference_block.title: Extended Primitives
reference_block.statement: These primitives extend the core set. They are used in specific phases, tasks, and disciplines and are fully governed by the same rules that apply to primitives 1–8.

reference_block.name: unknown
reference_block.title: Unknown
reference_block.statement: A question or area that the team cannot yet answer with available evidence. Distinct from an Assumption (which states a belief) — an Unknown records what is not yet known at all.

checklist.entry: Why it matters (impact if left unresolved)
How it will be resolved (planned research, analysis, or decision)
BA: capturing knowledge gaps before analysis
PM: surfacing risks to phase progression

reference_block.name: stakeholder-map
reference_block.title: Stakeholder Map
reference_block.statement: A structured view of everyone affected by, involved in, or with authority over a service or problem area. Used to ensure all relevant voices are considered before commitment.

checklist.entry: Interest or stake in the outcome
BA: problem capture and options appraisal
SD: co-design and service context mapping

reference_block.name: control
reference_block.title: Control
reference_block.statement: A formal mechanism — approval gate, spend limit, or governance checkpoint — that must be satisfied before an action can proceed. Controls are external constraints, not internal risks.

checklist.entry: PM: spend controls and phase gate authorisation

reference_block.name: gap
reference_block.title: Gap
reference_block.statement: A missing element between current state and a required or expected state. Used to surface what is absent — in evidence, in coverage, in capability, or in assurance.

checklist.entry: UR: evidence gaps in landscape scans
All roles: assurance gaps against Service Standard criteria

reference_block.name: insight-statement
reference_block.title: Insight Statement
reference_block.statement: A clear, evidence-backed statement about user behaviour, need, or experience that goes beyond raw data. An Insight Statement interprets evidence — it is not a finding alone.

checklist.entry: Implication for design, policy, or service
UR: synthesis outputs and research reports
PM: improvement prioritisation in live services

reference_block.name: risk-limitation
reference_block.title: Risk / Limitation
reference_block.statement: A risk to the validity, scope, or reliability of research or evidence — not a risk to delivery (which is covered by Risk or Constraint). Used specifically when surfacing the boundaries of what research can conclude.

checklist.entry: Description (what compromises the finding or conclusion)
Impact on confidence in the output
Mitigation or caveat to apply when using the evidence

reference_block.name: trade-off
reference_block.title: Trade-off
reference_block.statement: An explicit statement that choosing one option or approach means accepting a cost or reduction in another dimension. Trade-offs must be named, not implied.

checklist.entry: Conditions under which this trade-off is acceptable
Who is most affected by the cost
Reversibility (can the trade-off be revisited later)
PM: roadmap and backlog trade-off framing

reference_block.name: metric
reference_block.title: Metric
reference_block.statement: A quantifiable measure used to assess performance, success, or service health. Metrics must have a defined data source and known limitations.

checklist.entry: Known limitations (what the metric cannot tell you)
Metrics: performance analysis and service health

reference_block.name: limitation
reference_block.title: Limitation
reference_block.statement: A known boundary on what a piece of evidence, a measure, or a finding can conclude. Distinct from Risk — a Limitation describes the scope of knowledge, not a threat to delivery.

checklist.entry: What is limited (scope of the evidence or measure)
Why (method, data quality, time period, sample)
Impact on the conclusions that can be drawn
What additional work would reduce the limitation
PM: alpha success criteria (what alpha will not prove)

reference_block.name: mitigation
reference_block.title: Mitigation
reference_block.statement: An action taken or planned to reduce the likelihood or impact of an identified Risk or Constraint. A Mitigation does not eliminate a risk — it produces a Residual Risk.

checklist.entry: Linked risk (reference to Risk or Constraint)
Effectiveness (eliminates / significantly reduces / partially reduces the risk)
PM: live readiness and operational readiness

reference_block.name: residual-risk
reference_block.title: Residual Risk
reference_block.statement: The risk that remains after one or more Mitigations have been applied. Residual Risk must be explicitly named and accepted by an owner — it cannot be left implicit.

checklist.entry: Linked risk (reference to original Risk or Constraint)
Acceptance decision (who accepts it and the basis for acceptance)

reference_block.name: standard-reference
reference_block.title: Standard Reference
reference_block.statement: A specific criterion, requirement, or expectation drawn from a formal standard, framework, or regulation that applies to the service. Used to ground assurance evidence in named external requirements rather than general claims.

checklist.entry: Standard name (for example GDS Service Standard point 5, WCAG 2.2 criterion 1.4.3)
How it applies to this service
PM: service assurance and beta evidence packs
QA: acceptance criteria and test coverage
Accessibility: WCAG and accessibility statement evidence

reference_block.name: opportunity
reference_block.title: Opportunity
reference_block.statement: A potential area for meaningful improvement in a live service, identified from evidence but not yet committed to. Distinct from a Recommendation — an Opportunity surfaces a candidate for action without directing it.

checklist.entry: Evidence base (linked Evidence Units or Insight Statements)
Relative priority (compared to other open Opportunities)
PM: live improvement identification and backlog shaping
UR: synthesis of live research findings

reference_block.name: trend
reference_block.title: Trend
reference_block.statement: A directional pattern in a Metric or operational indicator over a defined time period. A Trend is an interpretation of data movement — it must distinguish signal from noise.

checklist.entry: Confidence in the pattern (signal vs noise assessment)

reference_block.name: cause
reference_block.title: Cause
reference_block.statement: An identified contributing factor to an incident, failure, or unexpected outcome. Causes are system-level — they must describe conditions, not attribute blame to individuals.

checklist.entry: Description (what happened, not who caused it)
Contributing system conditions that enabled it

reference_block.name: learning
reference_block.title: Learning
reference_block.statement: An actionable insight gained from an incident, experiment, or research event that changes or should change how the team works. Distinct from an Insight Statement — a Learning is directed at team practice, not service design.

checklist.entry: Who it is most relevant to

reference_block.name: scenario
reference_block.title: Scenario
reference_block.statement: A plausible future state that could affect the service, team, or users. Distinct from an Assumption (a belief to be tested) and a Risk (a threat to delivery) — a Scenario describes a possible future, which may be positive or negative, and is used to explore preparedness and response options.

checklist.entry: Trigger conditions (what would cause this scenario to materialise)
Likelihood (high / medium / low — with evidence basis)
Response options (what could be done if this scenario materialises)
PM: roadmap scenario planning and contingency
BA: options appraisal and sensitivity analysis
Sec: threat modelling and attack scenarios
DevOps: failure mode and capacity scenarios

reference_block.name: dependency
reference_block.title: Dependency
reference_block.statement: A concrete external factor that the work relies on. Distinct from a Constraint (which is fixed and non-negotiable) and a Risk (which is probabilistic) — a Dependency is a specific thing or action controlled by someone else that must be available or complete for work to proceed.

checklist.entry: Mitigation (fallback if the dependency is not met)
PM: delivery planning and dependency mapping
Ops: operational readiness and support dependencies
Dev: integration dependencies and third-party services

reference_block.name: design-decision
reference_block.title: Design Decision
reference_block.statement: A record of a decision that has been made by a human decision-maker. The agent may populate this primitive only when the user states that a decision has been made. The agent must never generate its own Design Decisions.

checklist.entry: Rationale (why this option was chosen — as stated by the decision-maker)
Review conditions (when or under what circumstances to revisit)
Owner (who made this decision — always a named human or governance body)
SD: recording design decisions that shape the service
PM: product decisions that affect scope, priority, or direction
Dev: implementation decisions that affect code or infrastructure

reference_block.statement: Governance constraint: The agent must not populate this primitive from its own reasoning. If no human decision-maker is identified, the agent must refuse and request attribution. Enforcement: LINT-DESIGN-DECISION-001 (BLOCK severity).

checklist.entry: Roles apply them using their own methods and artefacts.
All cross‑discipline collaboration should reference these primitives explicitly.

reference_block.name: non-goals
reference_block.title: Non‑Goals
reference_block.statement: Those belong in role‑specific or contextual files.

reference_block.name: design-intent
reference_block.title: Design Intent
reference_block.statement: These primitives act as the shared language of multidisciplinary delivery.
Think of this file as the grammar, and role files as dialects.

reference_block.name: ai-adoption-levels
reference_block.title: AI adoption levels
reference_block.statement: These primitives describe the depth and ambition of AI use. They must be used when the agent reasons about or explains AI‑related recommendations.

checklist.entry: Using AI to streamline or remove low‑value, repeatable, or purely administrative tasks
Typical effects: time saved, fewer manual steps, reduced error on routine work
No fundamental change to service scope or operating model
Using AI to change how a team, product or workflow operates locally
Typical effects: new ways of triaging, analysing, prioritising or supporting decisions; improved flow and quality
May affect roles, responsibilities and daily routines, but within existing service boundaries
Using AI to redesign end‑to‑end services, journeys or systems across organisational boundaries
Typical effects: new operating models, re‑shaped user journeys, new capabilities that could not exist without AI
Requires cross‑team, cross‑portfolio or cross‑organisation collaboration, governance and investment

reference_block.statement: When generating recommendations, the agent should:

checklist.entry: tag its own reasoning with one or more of these primitives
avoid presenting @adopt-ai changes as if they were @transform-with-ai
highlight when moving from one level to another will require new governance, skills, data or funding

reference_block.name: automation-and-productivity-primitives
reference_block.title: Automation and productivity primitives
reference_block.statement: These primitives describe how the agent reasons about value and change over time.

checklist.entry: @efficiency-gain – Doing the same work with fewer inputs (for example time, money, steps) and the same quality
@productivity-gain – Delivering better outcomes or more value per unit of input (for example fewer complaints, safer decisions, reduced failure demand)
@cost-cutting-risk – Reducing inputs (for example headcount, budget) without clear protection of quality, equity or outcomes
@transition-cost – Additional effort during change (for example learning, dual running, rework, process redesign) that may temporarily reduce productivity
@automation-j-curve – The pattern where productivity dips in the short term before recovering to a higher level after successful change

reference_block.statement: The agent should use these primitives to:

checklist.entry: explain the difference between short‑term efficiency and long‑term productivity
make transition costs explicit in plans and recommendations
flag when a proposal is primarily @cost-cutting-risk rather than a genuine @productivity-gain

reference_block.name: barrier-taxonomy
reference_block.title: Barrier taxonomy
reference_block.statement: These primitives classify the main types of barriers teams encounter when adopting or scaling AI. They help the agent move from problem description to targeted support.

checklist.entry: Constraints or uncertainty arising from law, regulation, policy, or formal governance (for example DPIAs, approval boards, statutory limits)
Issues relating to data access, quality, linkage, classification or sharing agreements
Lack of access to approved tools, licences or infrastructure; inconsistent rollout; or dependence on “shadow” tools
Gaps in skills, confidence, or time available to learn and experiment; over‑stretched teams with no capacity to change how they work
Misaligned incentives, fear of punishment if things go wrong, lack of recognition for improvement, or norms that favour the status quo

reference_block.statement: When the user describes a problem, the agent should:

checklist.entry: classify it using one or more of these primitives
use the classification to propose targeted next steps, such as:
what evidence or artefacts to prepare
what small, low‑risk tests could move things forward

reference_block.name: risk-and-ethics-primitives
reference_block.title: Risk and ethics primitives
reference_block.statement: These primitives allow the agent to talk about risk in a structured way, especially for high‑stakes uses.

checklist.entry: @service-quality-risk – Risk that AI degrades service quality, timeliness, accessibility or user experience
@fairness-bias-risk – Risk that AI embeds or worsens unfair treatment or bias across protected or vulnerable groups
@moral-injury-risk – Risk that staff are asked to implement or oversee decisions that conflict with their ethical judgement or professional standards
@accountability-risk – Risk that it becomes unclear who is responsible for decisions, or that decisions cannot be explained or challenged
@security-privacy-risk – Risk of data exposure, misuse, unauthorised access or loss of confidentiality
@shadow-ai-risk – Risk arising from use of unapproved tools, personal accounts or devices for official work
use these primitives to annotate its own reasoning where relevant
recommend mitigation strategies that keep humans meaningfully involved in high‑stakes decisions
what the AI or automation is doing
how people can challenge or escalate concerns

reference_block.name: worker-voice-and-engagement-primitives
reference_block.title: Worker voice and engagement primitives
reference_block.statement: These primitives help the agent reason about and encourage meaningful involvement of staff and partners.

checklist.entry: @frontline-insight – Knowledge and experience from people delivering or supporting the service day‑to‑day
@staff-engagement-opportunity – A concrete chance to involve staff in shaping AI use (for example workshops, pilots, retrospectives)
@union-partnership-opportunity – Situations where engagement with recognised staff representatives is appropriate and helpful
@feedback-loop – Mechanisms for capturing, acting on and closing the loop on staff and user feedback about AI use

reference_block.statement: When suggesting AI‑related changes, the agent should:

checklist.entry: recommend simple, proportionate @feedback-loop mechanisms that fit the team’s context

reference_block.name: powerpointmastertemplate
reference_block.title: PowerPointMasterTemplate
reference_block.statement: PowerPointMasterTemplate is the mandated Home Office PowerPoint master template used for all PowerPoint artefact generation by the Product Assistant Agent.

reference_block.name: source-of-truth
reference_block.title: Source of truth
reference_block.statement: The definition, location, and governance of this template are defined exclusively in:
No other file may redefine, override, or substitute this template.

checklist.entry: Slide master name: “Slide template for presentations — [Presenter] Home Office Digital”

reference_block.name: required-behaviour
reference_block.title: Required behaviour
reference_block.statement: When generating a PowerPoint artefact, the agent must:

checklist.entry: Load the PowerPointMasterTemplate before any content is drafted
Create a copy of the template
Delete all existing slides from the copied file
Create new slides only using layouts from this template’s slide masters
Resolve layouts by master name and layout name, never by index
Treat any missing required layout as a hard error
Append slides to the original template
Fall back silently to a default layout
Create content before the template is loaded

reference_block.name: enforcement
reference_block.title: Enforcement
reference_block.statement: Use of PowerPointMasterTemplate is mandatory and enforced via agent-lint.config.xml when a PowerPoint artefact is produced.

reference_block.name: forbidden-reasoning-patterns-anti-primitives
reference_block.title: Forbidden Reasoning Patterns (Anti‑Primitives)
reference_block.statement: These patterns represent reasoning outputs that the agent must not produce in certain contexts. They are used as forbidden_primitives in references/task-catalog.xml to prevent the agent from exceeding its advisory authority.
Forbidden patterns are governed by the same enforcement model as permitted primitives. The lint specification (LINT-TASK-004) blocks responses containing forbidden patterns for the active task.

reference_block.name: decision
reference_block.title: Decision
reference_block.statement: A definitive commitment to a course of action. Distinct from a Recommendation — a Decision closes a choice rather than informing one. The agent is advisory and must never close choices on behalf of the user.

checklist.entry: Presenting a single path as settled rather than recommended

reference_block.statement: Permitted alternative: Recommendation (with linked evidence, risks, and dependencies)

reference_block.name: approval
reference_block.title: Approval
reference_block.statement: A statement that work, a design, a plan, or an artefact has been formally accepted or signed off. Approval authority belongs to humans, not the agent.

checklist.entry: Implying that the agent's review constitutes formal acceptance

reference_block.statement: Permitted alternative: Assessment or Gap analysis (describing what has been reviewed, with confidence and residual risk)

reference_block.name: commitment
reference_block.title: Commitment
reference_block.statement: A binding statement that resources, timelines, scope, or delivery outcomes are fixed. Commitments constrain future action and belong to accountable humans.

checklist.entry: "The team will…" (as a binding statement, not a suggestion)
Language that forecloses options without explicit human authorisation

reference_block.statement: Permitted alternative: Option or Outcome (describing intended change, success measures, and review points without binding language)

reference_block.name: blame
reference_block.title: Blame
reference_block.statement: Attribution of a failure, incident, or negative outcome to a named individual or team. The agent must describe systemic conditions and contributing factors, not assign personal responsibility.

checklist.entry: "The team failed to…" (implying culpability rather than describing conditions)
Language that attributes fault rather than identifying causes

reference_block.statement: Permitted alternative: Cause (describing system‑level contributing factors without individual attribution)

reference_block.name: selection
reference_block.title: Selection
reference_block.statement: A statement that one option has been chosen over others. Distinct from a Recommendation — a Selection removes alternatives from consideration. Only humans may select.

checklist.entry: "The preferred option is…" (when presented as final, not as a recommendation)
Narrowing to a single option without flagging this as a recommendation requiring human confirmation

reference_block.statement: Permitted alternative: Recommendation (with trade‑off analysis and explicit statement that selection is human‑owned)

reference_block.name: instruction
reference_block.title: Instruction
reference_block.statement: A directive to implement, build, deploy, or take operational action. The agent may advise on how something could be done but must not direct that it be done.

checklist.entry: "Do X" (as a directive, not as guidance)
Imperative language directed at delivery teams without framing as advice

reference_block.statement: Permitted alternative: Recommendation or Option (describing what could be done, with risks and dependencies, framed as advice)

reference_block.name: roadmap
reference_block.title: Roadmap
reference_block.statement: A committed sequence of delivery milestones with implied resource and timeline commitments. In early phases (Pre‑Discovery, Discovery), roadmap language is premature and creates false confidence.

checklist.entry: "Phase 1: … Phase 2: … Phase 3: …" (with delivery dates or implied sequencing commitments)
Presenting a sequence of work as planned rather than as a possible approach

reference_block.statement: Permitted alternative: Option (describing possible sequencing with trade‑offs and unknowns) or Outcome (describing intended results without committing to a delivery sequence)

reference_block.name: certification
reference_block.title: Certification
reference_block.statement: A statement that a service, product, or artefact has been formally assessed and found to meet a standard. Certification authority belongs to assessors and governing bodies, not the agent.

checklist.entry: Any language implying formal compliance status

reference_block.statement: Permitted alternative: Standard Reference with Gap analysis (describing which criteria have evidence, which have gaps, and what remains to be assessed)
